findings.title: EFS Security Group Allows All Egress
findings.issue: The EFS security group allows all outbound traffic to 0.0.0.0/0 on all ports and protocols. EFS mount targets only need inbound NFS (port 2049) and do not initiate outbound connections.
findings.impact: Minimal direct risk, but defense-in-depth suggests removing unnecessary egress from the EFS security group.
findings.recommendation: Remove the egress block entirely from the EFS security group. EFS mount targets are passive listeners and require no outbound rules.

findings.title: Example Script Uses assignPublicIp=ENABLED
findings.issue: The example launch script uses assignPublicIp=ENABLED when launching Fargate tasks. The production Lambda handler correctly uses DISABLED.
findings.impact: If an operator copies this example script for production use, Fargate tasks would receive public IP addresses, exposing the SSM agent and container services to the internet.
findings.recommendation: Change to assignPublicIp=DISABLED in the example script, or add a prominent warning comment.

findings.title: oc_version Not Validated in Lambda Handler
findings.issue: The oc_version parameter from the request body is accepted without validation and passed directly into the task definition environment variables and task tags. The handler validates investigation_id and cluster_id but not oc_version against the set of valid versions {4.14, 4.15, 4.16, 4.17, 4.18, 4.19, 4.20}.
findings.impact: A malicious or errant client can inject arbitrary strings into the OC_VERSION environment variable and task tags. The entrypoint validates whether the path exists, so invalid versions fall back to default, but the unvalidated tag value pollutes audit tagging.

findings.title: Terraform AWS Provider Broadly Pinned and Lockfile Gitignored
findings.issue: The AWS provider is pinned with version = "~> 6.0", which allows any 6.x release. The .terraform.lock.hcl is explicitly gitignored in both deploy/regional/.gitignore and the root .gitignore, meaning different Terraform runs can use different provider versions with no lock enforcement.
findings.impact: A compromised or buggy provider release within the 6.x range could be silently adopted on the next terraform init. Without a committed lockfile, supply chain integrity of the provider binary is not verifiable in CI.
findings.recommendation: Pin to a narrower range (e.g., "~> 6.80") and remove .terraform.lock.hcl from .gitignore so it is committed and verified in CI.

findings.title: UBI9 Base Image Pinned by Tag, Not Digest
findings.issue: The base image FROM registry.access.redhat.com/ubi9/ubi is pinned to a floating tag. The ubi9/ubi tag is updated in-place when Red Hat releases security patches, so rebuilds can silently pull a different base layer.
findings.impact: Rebuilds could silently pull a different base image with different packages. For a security-sensitive SRE tooling container used in production, reproducible builds matter.
findings.recommendation:
Pin to an immutable digest:

FROM registry.access.redhat.com/ubi9/ubi@sha256:<digest>

findings.title: Claude Code Installed via Piped Shell Script Without Integrity Verification
findings.issue: Claude Code is installed by piping a remote script to bash: curl -fsSL https://claude.ai/install.sh | INSTALL_DIR=/usr/local/lib/claude-code BIN_DIR=/usr/local/bin bash. No checksum or signature verification is performed on the installer.
findings.impact: A compromised CDN or MITM at build time could inject malicious code into the container image. The -fsSL flags use HTTPS which reduces MITM risk, but there is no integrity gate.
findings.recommendation: If Claude Code publishes checksums or release signatures, verify them after download. Alternatively, pre-stage the installer in a trusted internal registry and pull from there.

findings.title: Dependabot Configured Only for Docker, Not Go Modules or Python Dependencies
findings.category: Supply Chain — Missing Dependency Update Coverage
findings.issue: The Dependabot configuration monitors only the Docker package ecosystem. Go module dependencies (go.mod/go.sum) and Python Lambda dependencies (lambda/create-investigation/pyproject.toml) are not covered by automated update PRs.
findings.impact: Known vulnerabilities in Go dependencies (e.g., aws-sdk-go-v2, cobra, viper) or Python dependencies (e.g., PyJWT, cryptography, requests) will not surface automatically as PRs. Security patches require manual tracking.
findings.recommendation:
Add gomod and pip (or pip-compile) ecosystems to dependabot.yml:

  - package-ecosystem: "gomod"
    directory: "/"
    schedule:
      interval: "weekly"
  - package-ecosystem: "pip"
    directory: "/lambda/create-investigation"
    schedule:
      interval: "weekly"

findings.title: CloudWatch Log Groups Missing KMS Encryption
findings.issue: The two CloudWatch log groups — /ecs/${project}-${stage} (container logs) and /ecs/${project}-${stage}/ssm-sessions (SSM session logs) — are created without kms_key_id. The KMS key aws_kms_key.exec_session is already configured with a CloudWatch Logs principal in its key policy, so the infrastructure is ready for CMK encryption but it is not applied to the log groups themselves.
findings.impact: Container stdout/stderr and SSM session command logs are stored unencrypted at rest in CloudWatch Logs (using AWS-managed default encryption). An AWS support engineer with CloudWatch access or a lateral-movement compromise could read investigation session logs without triggering KMS audit events.
findings.recommendation:
Add kms_key_id to both log group resources:

resource "aws_cloudwatch_log_group" "rosa_boundary" {
  name              = "/ecs/${var.project}-${var.stage}"
  retention_in_days = var.log_retention_days
  kms_key_id        = aws_kms_key.exec_session.arn
  tags = local.common_tags
}

findings.title: Lambda ECS IAM Policy Uses Wildcard Resource
findings.issue: The Lambda IAM policy grants ecs:RunTask, ecs:StopTask, ecs:ListTasks, ecs:DescribeTasks, ecs:DescribeTaskDefinition, ecs:RegisterTaskDefinition, ecs:DeregisterTaskDefinition, and ecs:TagResource on Resource = "*". This allows the Lambda function to manage tasks and task definitions across any ECS cluster in the account.
findings.impact: If the Lambda is compromised via a token validation bypass or dependency supply chain attack, the attacker can launch, stop, or register task definitions in any cluster in the AWS account.
findings.recommendation:
Scope ECS resources to the specific cluster. Note that RegisterTaskDefinition and DeregisterTaskDefinition do not support resource-level IAM scoping and must retain *, but RunTask, StopTask, ListTasks, DescribeTasks, and TagResource can be scoped:

Resource = [
  aws_ecs_cluster.main.arn,
  "arn:${data.aws_partition.current.partition}:ecs:${data.aws_region.current.name}:${data.aws_caller_identity.current.account_id}:task/${aws_ecs_cluster.main.name}/*",
  "arn:${data.aws_partition.current.partition}:ecs:${data.aws_region.current.name}:${data.aws_caller_identity.current.account_id}:task-definition/${var.project}-${var.stage}*"
]

findings.title: Shared SRE Role Grants KMS Decrypt on All Keys
findings.issue: The KMSForECSExec statement in the shared SRE role's ABAC policy grants kms:Decrypt and kms:GenerateDataKey on Resource = "*". Any SRE who assumes this role can use any KMS key in the account, subject only to key policies.
findings.impact: An SRE could decrypt data encrypted with any CMK that has a permissive key policy. The task role (iam.tf) correctly scopes to aws_kms_key.exec_session.arn, making this gap in the shared SRE role an inconsistency.
findings.recommendation:
Scope the KMS resource to the exec session key:

Resource = aws_kms_key.exec_session.arn

findings.title: CloudWatch Logs Encryption Disabled for ECS Exec Sessions
findings.issue: The ECS cluster execute command configuration sets cloud_watch_encryption_enabled = false. While a KMS key is referenced for session-level encryption (kms_key_id = aws_kms_key.exec_session.arn), the CloudWatch log group storing SSM session logs does not use CMK encryption.
findings.impact: SSM session logs may contain sensitive commands and output from SRE investigations. Without CMK encryption on the log group, there is no independent audit trail for key usage and no ability to revoke access to historical logs via key policy.

findings.title: S3 Audit Bucket Uses SSE-S3 Instead of SSE-KMS
findings.issue: The S3 audit bucket uses sse_algorithm = "AES256" (SSE-S3) instead of aws:kms (SSE-KMS). SSE-S3 uses AWS-managed keys with no audit trail for key usage and no ability to control access via KMS key policies.
findings.impact: For an audit log bucket with WORM compliance (Object Lock), the inability to independently audit encryption key usage or enforce key-level access controls weakens the security posture. Any principal with s3:GetObject permission can decrypt objects; there is no separate kms:Decrypt gate.

findings.title: No EFS Filesystem Policy Enforcing IAM Authorization
findings.issue: The EFS filesystem has no aws_efs_file_system_policy resource. While the access points require IAM (iam = "ENABLED" in the task definition), the filesystem itself does not enforce IAM authorization or deny anonymous access. Any entity with network access to the mount targets (port 2049) could potentially mount the filesystem directly without an access point.
findings.impact: If the Fargate security group or VPC network controls are misconfigured, an attacker with network access to the NFS port could mount the EFS filesystem as root and access all investigation data across all access points.

findings.title: S3 Audit Bucket Missing Deny-Non-TLS Policy and Access Logging
findings.category: Infrastructure — Missing Access Control and Auditability
findings.issue: The S3 audit bucket has no aws_s3_bucket_policy to enforce encryption in transit (deny non-TLS requests). It also has no S3 server access logging configured for the bucket itself.
findings.impact: Without a deny-non-TLS policy, misconfigured clients or proxies could theoretically write over unencrypted HTTP. Without server access logging, unauthorized access attempts to audit data would not be independently recorded.

findings.title: Lambda CORS Allows All Origins
findings.issue: The Lambda function URL CORS configuration sets allow_origins = ["*"] with a comment 'Allow localhost for testing; restrict in production' — but this is not stage-gated and applies to all environments.
findings.impact: A malicious website could make cross-origin requests to the Lambda function URL. The primary auth mechanism is SigV4 (which cannot be replicated cross-origin), but the wildcard CORS combined with Access-Control-Allow-Headers: x-oidc-token means any page with a valid OIDC token could invoke the function.
findings.recommendation: Restrict CORS origins to known SRE tool domains or remove CORS entirely since the CLI uses direct SDK invocation rather than browser-based HTTP.

findings.title: Lambda CORS Allows All Origins
findings.issue: The Lambda handler response function sets Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * on every response, including 200 success responses containing task ARNs and role ARNs.
findings.impact: Sensitive response fields (task_arn, role_arn, access_point_id) are returned with a wildcard CORS header, allowing any cross-origin script that obtains a valid OIDC token to read these values.
findings.recommendation: Match the CORS origin header to a configured allowlist rather than reflecting *. If the function is only called via CLI SDK invocation, remove the CORS headers entirely.

findings.title: Container Grants Passwordless Sudo ALL
findings.issue: The SRE user is granted NOPASSWD: ALL sudo access: echo 'sre ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: ALL' > /etc/sudoers.d/sre. This allows any user who connects via ECS Exec to escalate to root inside the container.
findings.impact: A compromised or malicious SRE session can escalate to root inside the container. Root access enables modification of entrypoint/cleanup scripts (bypassing S3 audit sync), installing additional tools, or attempting to use the task role IAM credentials for lateral movement. The entrypoint already runs as root and handles alternatives switching, so this breadth of sudo is unnecessary.
findings.recommendation:
Restrict sudo to the minimum commands the sre user requires (e.g., alternatives only):

sre ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: /usr/sbin/alternatives

If the entrypoint handles all root operations at startup, the sre user may need no sudo at all.

findings.title: Python Lambda Dependencies Use >= Version Pinning in pyproject.toml
findings.issue: Lambda dependencies use >= specifiers in pyproject.toml: PyJWT>=2.8.0, cryptography>=41.0.0, requests>=2.31.0, boto3>=1.34.0. A uv.lock file exists and is committed, which does pin the actual resolved versions used by the build toolchain. However, the pyproject.toml itself does not reflect exact pinned versions, so alternative install paths (pip install -r, direct pip install .) would pull current-latest rather than the locked versions.
findings.impact: If the Lambda is built via a path that bypasses uv.lock (e.g., pip install from pyproject.toml directly), a compromised or buggy version of PyJWT, cryptography, or requests could be silently installed. The cryptography and PyJWT packages are high-value targets for supply chain attacks.
findings.recommendation: Change pyproject.toml to use == pinning matching the uv.lock resolved versions, and ensure the Lambda build Makefile always uses uv sync --frozen to enforce the lockfile.

findings.title: Bedrock IAM Policy Allows All Regions and All Models
findings.issue: The Bedrock policy on the task role grants bedrock:InvokeModel and bedrock:InvokeModelWithResponseStream on arn:${partition}:bedrock:*:*:inference-profile/* and arn:${partition}:bedrock:*:*:foundation-model/*. The wildcard regions and all-model access mean the task role can invoke any foundation model in any region.
findings.impact: If an SRE container is compromised, the attacker can invoke arbitrary Bedrock models in any region, leading to cost abuse. Data sent to Bedrock models outside the expected region may also violate data residency requirements.
findings.recommendation:
Scope to the specific region and model family:

Resource = [
  "arn:${data.aws_partition.current.partition}:bedrock:${data.aws_region.current.name}::foundation-model/anthropic.claude-*",
  "arn:${data.aws_partition.current.partition}:bedrock:${data.aws_region.current.name}:${data.aws_caller_identity.current.account_id}:inference-profile/*"
]

findings.title: Lambda IAM Policy Missing elasticfilesystem:DeleteAccessPoint Permission Causes Silent Cleanup Failures
findings.issue: The Terraform EFS policy was updated to include elasticfilesystem:DeleteAccessPoint (line 82 now includes it). However, the handler still uses bare except: pass blocks around cleanup calls, silently swallowing any failures. Verify via IAM policy simulator that the deployed Lambda execution role actually has DeleteAccessPoint on the specific filesystem ARN.
findings.impact: Silent cleanup failures leave orphaned EFS access points. An attacker who can repeatedly trigger task launch failures could accumulate access points approaching the 10,000-per-filesystem limit, causing a denial-of-service for all investigations.
findings.recommendation: Ensure bare except: pass blocks in all four cleanup paths are replaced with explicit logging. Add a CloudWatch alarm on Lambda error rate and an access point count metric approaching the 10,000 limit.

findings.title: kube-proxy Sidecar Writes Kubeconfig to Shared Ephemeral Volume Readable by SRE User
findings.issue: When the kube-proxy sidecar is enabled, its startup command writes the cluster kubeconfig to /tmp/kubeconfig inside the proxy-tmp ephemeral volume: printf '%s' "$KUBECONFIG_DATA" > /tmp/kubeconfig. The rosa-boundary container's entrypoint also writes a kubeconfig pointing to localhost:${KUBE_PROXY_PORT} at /home/sre/.kube/config. The secret is injected at container launch via ECS Secrets Manager injection (KUBECONFIG_DATA), but then stored as a plaintext file accessible within the container.
findings.impact: The sre user (and any process running as sre) can read the kubeconfig from /home/sre/.kube/config, which grants API access to the ROSA cluster via the proxy. More critically, the kubeconfig in /tmp/kubeconfig within the proxy-tmp volume is writable by the kube-proxy process. If the proxy container is compromised, an attacker could overwrite the kubeconfig to redirect API calls.
findings.recommendation: Use readOnlyRootFilesystem on the kube-proxy sidecar (already set) but additionally: (1) Set the kubeconfig file to read-only after creation (chmod 400), (2) Use a named pipe or env-var injection instead of a file where possible, (3) Restrict the S3 sync in entrypoint.sh from syncing .kube/ contents to audit storage.

findings.title: Adversary Agent Missing Explicit File-Write Scope Constraint
findings.issue: The Output Format section instructs the agent to write findings to adversary-findings.json, but the agent definition contains no explicit constraint preventing it from writing to other files. With unrestricted Bash access, a prompt injection could cause the agent to overwrite project source files, CI configuration, or secrets.
findings.impact: A manipulated agent could overwrite deploy/regional/*.tf, .github/workflows/*.yml, or entrypoint.sh, introducing backdoors into infrastructure definitions or CI pipelines. The SARIF upload workflow would then surface the tampered content as an authorized security scan result.
findings.recommendation: Add explicit guardrails to the agent definition: 'Do NOT write to any file other than adversary-findings.json at the repository root. Do NOT modify source code, infrastructure files, CI/CD configurations, or any file in deploy/, lambda/, .github/, or internal/. Do NOT execute git push, git commit, or any command that modifies remote state.'

findings.title: GitHub Actions Workflows Use Mutable Action Tags Without SHA Pinning
findings.issue: All GitHub Actions in both workflow files are pinned to mutable semantic version tags (@v6, @v2, @v7, @v4) rather than immutable commit SHAs. Actions used: actions/checkout@v6, actions/setup-python@v6, EnricoMi/publish-unit-test-result-action/composite@v2, codecov/codecov-action@v7, github/codeql-action/upload-sarif@v4. A tag can be force-pushed by the action author or a compromised account to point to malicious code.
findings.impact: A supply chain attack on any of these action repositories could inject malicious steps into CI, enabling exfiltration of the LOCALSTACK_AUTH_TOKEN secret, modification of adversary-findings.json before SARIF upload, or compromise of the runner environment.
findings.recommendation:
Pin all actions to a specific commit SHA:

- uses: actions/checkout@11bd71901bbe5b1630ceea73d27597364c9af683  # v4.2.2

Use a tool like `pin-github-actions` or Dependabot's `Actions` ecosystem to maintain SHA pins automatically.

findings.title: Lambda Runtime Python Version Mismatches CI Test Environment
findings.issue: Both Lambda functions are deployed with runtime = "python3.11" but CI runs tests under Python 3.14 (setup-python with python-version: '3.14'). Python 3.14 has breaking changes in type annotation behavior and some stdlib modules, meaning tests pass on 3.14 but could fail or behave differently at runtime on 3.11.
findings.impact: Security-relevant behavior differences between Python 3.11 and 3.14 could go undetected in CI. For example, datetime.fromisoformat() in the reaper lambda has subtly different behavior between versions. A bug masked by version differences could produce incorrect deadline parsing and allow tasks to run past their deadline.
findings.recommendation: Align CI Python version with Lambda runtime. Set python-version: '3.11' in both workflow jobs, or update the Lambda runtime to python3.12 (the latest supported Lambda runtime as of 2026) and use that in CI.

findings.title: uv Installer Piped from the Internet in CI Without Integrity Verification
findings.issue: Both CI jobs install uv by piping a remote shell script directly to sh: curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh. The installer is not verified against a checksum or signature before execution. The same pattern repeats in the lambda-unit-tests job.
findings.impact: A compromised astral.sh CDN or DNS hijack could inject malicious code that runs with full runner privileges during CI. The runner has access to the LOCALSTACK_AUTH_TOKEN secret and the GitHub Actions token, making this a high-value target for secret exfiltration.
findings.recommendation:
Use the official setup-uv GitHub Action (astral-sh/setup-uv@v6) which handles verification, or download and verify the installer against a published SHA256 checksum:

- name: Install uv
  uses: astral-sh/setup-uv@v6
  with:
    version: "0.7.x"

findings.title: OIDC ID Token Cached to Disk in Plaintext
findings.issue: The Go CLI caches the Keycloak OIDC ID token as a plaintext file at ~/.cache/rosa-boundary/token-cache with mode 0600. The cache validity period is 4 minutes. The token grants access to the Lambda invoker role and, after role assumption, to the shared SRE role which can ECS Exec into investigation tasks.
findings.impact: An attacker with read access to the SRE's home directory (local compromise, misconfigured umask, or unauthorized ~/.cache access) can extract a valid OIDC token within its 4-minute window. With this token they can call the Lambda to create investigations or assume the shared SRE role. The 4-minute cache does not guarantee the token has not already been used to obtain longer-lived STS credentials cached elsewhere.
findings.recommendation: Store the cached token in the OS keychain (using the `keyring` pattern or `go-keychain`) rather than a plaintext file. If filesystem caching is required, consider encrypting the token with a key derived from a local secret (e.g., OS login keyring). At minimum, document the risk and provide a `rosa-boundary login --clear-cache` workflow for operators who work in shared or untrusted environments.

findings.title: Keycloak Deployed with hostname.strict: false
findings.issue: The Keycloak CR sets hostname.strict: false, which disables strict hostname validation. Keycloak will respond to requests with any Host header value rather than requiring a match to the configured hostname. This is a Keycloak production misconfiguration warning category.
findings.impact: With strict hostname validation disabled, an attacker who can send requests to the Keycloak pod (e.g., via DNS rebinding, a compromised internal service, or misconfigured ingress) using an arbitrary Host header can generate tokens with a redirect_uri pointing to an attacker-controlled domain. Depending on Keycloak OIDC client configuration, this could allow authorization code interception or token redirect attacks.
findings.recommendation:
Set hostname.strict: true and configure hostname.hostname to the canonical Keycloak URL:

hostname:
  hostname: https://keycloak.example.com
  strict: true

Also review and tighten the Valid Redirect URIs in the Keycloak OIDC client configuration to the exact SRE tool callback URL.

findings.title: Fargate Task Security Group Allows Unrestricted Egress
findings.issue: The Fargate task security group has a single egress rule allowing all traffic to 0.0.0.0/0 on all ports and protocols. Investigation containers have broad AWS API access (ECS Exec via SSM, S3 sync, Bedrock invocation) but unrestricted egress also permits arbitrary outbound connections from within the container.
findings.impact: A compromised SRE container or malicious CLAUDE.md instruction could exfiltrate investigation data (must-gather outputs, cluster credentials, AWS credentials from the task role) to an external host. The --no-follow-symlinks flag on S3 sync helps with file exfiltration via that path, but unrestricted egress provides an alternative channel.
findings.recommendation:
Restrict egress to known endpoints using VPC endpoints for SSM, S3, ECR, ECS, Bedrock, EFS, KMS, and CloudWatch Logs. Replace the catch-all egress rule with specific rules for:
- Port 443 to the VPC endpoints for required AWS services
- Port 2049 to the EFS security group (for NFS mount)
- Port 443 to known ROSA API server CIDR ranges if direct cluster access is required

findings.title: AWS Account ID Hardcoded in Keycloak IRSA Service Account Annotation
findings.category: Infrastructure — Sensitive Data in Source Control
findings.issue: The Keycloak external-secrets service account has the IAM role ARN hardcoded in its annotation: eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn: arn:aws:iam::641875867446:role/dev-keycloak. This exposes the AWS account ID (641875867446) in source control.
findings.impact: AWS account IDs are not considered secrets by themselves, but exposing them in public or semi-public repositories enables targeted account enumeration attacks. An attacker can use the known account ID to attempt cross-account IAM role assumption, enumerate public S3 buckets or AMIs, and craft more precise phishing or social engineering attacks against AWS support. If this repository is or becomes public, the account ID exposure is permanent in git history.
findings.recommendation:
Replace the hardcoded ARN with a Kustomize variable substitution or a separate secrets-managed reference that is not committed:

annotations:
  eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn: $(EXTERNAL_SECRETS_ROLE_ARN)

Alternatively, use an ExternalSecret or ConfigMap to inject the role ARN at deploy time from a parameter that is not stored in the repository.
